title: File Handling
description: Reading and writing files, context managers, working with CSV and JSON file formats, and path manipulation with the pathlib module.

sections.title: Reading & Writing Text Files
sections.description: Always use context managers (the 'with' block) to open files so they close automatically. Supports read, write, and append modes.
sections.codeBlock:
# Reading Text Files
with open("data.txt", "r") as f:
    content = f.read()          # Read entire file as str
    f.seek(0)                   # Reset file pointer
    first_line = f.readline()   # Read one line
    f.seek(0)
    lines = f.readlines()       # List of all lines

# Memory efficient iteration line-by-line
with open("data.txt") as f:
    for line in f:
        print(line.strip())

# Writing Text Files (overwrites existing)
with open("out.txt", "w") as f:
    f.write("Hello, World!\n")
    f.writelines(["line1\n", "line2\n"])

# Append Mode (adds to end of file)
with open("log.txt", "a") as f:
    f.write("new entry\n")

sections.title: Binary Files
sections.description: Append 'b' to the mode string (e.g., 'rb', 'wb') to read or write binary data, which returns raw bytes instead of strings.
sections.codeBlock:
# Read raw binary data
with open("image.jpg", "rb") as f:
    data = f.read()

# Write raw binary data
with open("copy.jpg", "wb") as f:
    f.write(data)

sections.title: JSON Serialization & Parsing
sections.description: Use the json module to save structured dictionaries to files (dump) or serialize them to in-memory strings (dumps).
sections.codeBlock:
import json

data = {"name": "Alice", "scores": [95, 88]}

# Write JSON to file
with open("data.json", "w") as f:
    json.dump(data, f, indent=2)

# Read JSON from file
with open("data.json") as f:
    loaded = json.load(f)

# In-memory JSON operations
json_str = json.dumps(data)     # Serialize to string
decoded = json.loads(json_str)   # Deserialize from string

sections.title: CSV Processing
sections.description: Read and write tabular data easily using the csv module. DictReader automatically maps fields to dictionaries based on the header row.
sections.codeBlock:
import csv

# Reading CSV with header mapping
with open("data.csv") as f:
    reader = csv.DictReader(f)
    for row in reader:
        print(row["name"], row["score"])

# Writing CSV
with open("out.csv", "w", newline="") as f:
    writer = csv.writer(f)
    writer.writerow(["name", "score"])
    writer.writerow(["Alice", 95])

sections.title: Modern Path Manipulation (pathlib)
sections.description: The pathlib module provides an object-oriented approach to paths. Paths joined with the '/' operator are cross-platform by default.
sections.codeBlock:
from pathlib import Path

# Create a path reference
p = Path("data/subdir/file.txt")
parent_dir = p.parent  # Path("data/subdir")
file_name = p.name     # "file.txt"
file_stem = p.stem     # "file"
file_ext = p.suffix    # ".txt"

# Check properties
exists = p.exists()    # True/False
is_file = p.is_file()
is_dir = p.is_dir()

# Read/Write Shortcuts (no 'with open' needed)
text = p.read_text()
p.write_text("new content")
binary_data = p.read_bytes()

# Directory iterations & creations
for item in Path(".").iterdir():
    print(item.name)

# Create directory recursively
Path("newdir/subdir").mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

details:
The open() function is the primary API for file I/O. It returns a file object with modes 'r' (read, default), 'w' (write, truncates), 'a' (append), 'x' (exclusive creation), and 'b' suffix for binary mode. Always use the with statement — it guarantees the file is closed even if an exception occurs, avoiding resource leaks.

The json module provides full RFC 7159 compliance. Use json.dump() to serialize to a file and json.dumps() to serialize to a string; the reverse operations are json.load() and json.loads(). Custom objects can be serialized by providing a default callable or defining a custom JSON encoder. For streaming large JSON documents, consider ijson or simdjson.

The csv module implements RFC 4180. csv.DictReader and csv.DictWriter use the first row as field names, making code more readable. The newline="" parameter when writing CSV files prevents embedded newlines in quoted fields from being misinterpreted. For complex CSV parsing, the pandas library offers significantly more power.

The pathlib module (Python 3.4+) provides object-oriented path manipulation. Path objects overload the / operator for path joining (Path("dir") / "subdir" / "file.txt"), making file paths cross-platform by default. Its read_text() and write_text() convenience methods eliminate boilerplate for common operations. Prefer pathlib over os.path for new code.

For XML handling, see xml.etree.ElementTree in the standard library. For Excel files, use openpyxl (XLSX) or xlrd (XLS). For configuration files, configparser handles INI-style formats, and tomllib (Python 3.11+) handles TOML.

See the file I/O tutorial, json module, csv module, and pathlib module documentation for complete details.
